one_line_takeaway: MPS reframes LLM provenance/IP-derivation detection as a set-valued statistical inference problem, using a sequential permutation test-and-exclusion procedure to return a compact candidate-model set with a provable (asymptotic) 1-alpha coverage guarantee, outperforming pairwise heuristic baselines (REEF, LLMPrint, LLMDNA, FWER) on a 455-model Hugging Face benchmark and the Bench-A attribution/detection tasks.
key_finding: On the paper's own 455-LLM Hugging Face benchmark (135M-3B parameters, lineage depth up to 3), MPS empirically maintains coverage above the theoretical 1-alpha bound across candidate-pool sizes |M| in [5,100] and across TAM (True Ancestor Model count) in {0,1,2}, while keeping predicted set sizes tightly concentrated near the ideal (ground-truth) size -- e.g., with 2 true related models among 50 candidates, MPS achieves 96% coverage with average predicted set size 2.1 at alpha=0.05. Coverage remains above 1-alpha even at low overhead (R=20 permutations, or T=15k prompts, for TAM=1), stabilizing once R>=100 and plateauing in prompt count between T=15k and 70k. In head-to-head comparison against four empirical baselines (REEF, LLMPrint, LLMDNA, FWER) on the human-curated Bench-A dataset extended to multi-candidate settings: MPS attribution accuracy exceeds 0.97 (Semantic-MPS) and 0.95 (Token-MPS) across all pool sizes, versus baselines that degrade sharply in the no-parent (TAM=0) case due to independent per-pair decisions inflating false positives; for unauthorized-derivative detection, MPS keeps Precision and Recall both above 0.97 across pool sizes, while REEF's Recall collapses to about 0.1 at |M|=50 because its any-hit decision rule accumulates false positives as the pool grows. The derived Non-Infringement Score (the p-value pM itself) stays above 0.85 for genuinely non-infringing cases (TAM=0) and drops to near-zero for infringing cases (TAM>0), evidencing a clear separating margin.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes 'model provenance' as a model-SET identification problem with a statistical coverage guarantee (rather than a pairwise yes/no fingerprint match), and proposes Model Provenance Set (MPS): a sequential test-and-exclusion procedure that, given a target LLM g and a candidate pool M of possible base/ancestor models, returns a small predicted set M-hat such that Pr(M* subset M-hat) >= 1-alpha, where M* is the true set of provenance (ancestor) models. MPS computes a studentized t-statistic per candidate measuring how much closer a candidate's outputs are to the target than the ensemble average, tests the minimum such statistic (Tmin) via a permutation test to get a p-value pM, and iteratively removes ('excludes') the most-similar candidate as long as pM <= alpha, stopping when the remaining candidates are statistically indistinguishable in closeness to g. It is fingerprint/distance-function agnostic (works with any similarity score s(.,.)) and comes with two theorems: asymptotic validity of the permutation p-value (Theorem 3.1) and a coverage guarantee Pr(M* subset M-hat) >= 1-alpha as N,R -> infinity (Theorem 3.2), plus an asymptotic-efficiency result (Theorem 3.3) showing MPS recovers M* exactly with probability >= 1 - O(exp(-cN*delta^2)) when there is a sufficient similarity gap delta between true and false provenance models.
threat_model: Not an attack/defense threat model in the adversarial sense; this is a statistical auditing/detection method. The application scenario is IP protection and provenance auditing: an auditor/platform holds a target model g and a pool of candidate base models M, and wants to determine (a) which specific models g was derived from (attribution), (b) whether g was derived from ANY of a set of models without authorization (binary risk detection), or (c) whether there is sufficient statistical evidence to support a non-infringement claim for g. The 'adversary' implicit in the motivating scenario is an entity that fine-tunes/quantizes/merges another party's LLM and redistributes it without disclosing derivation (unauthorized reuse/misattribution), but the paper itself does not model an adaptive attacker trying to evade MPS -- it assumes black-box behavioral access (next-token or response outputs) to candidate and target models and does not test evasion/adaptive-adversary robustness.
limitations: The coverage and efficiency guarantees (Theorems 3.1-3.3) are ASYMPTOTIC (as N, R -> infinity) and rely on regularity conditions (i.i.d. prompts, finite (2+delta)-moments, nonzero variance under the null); finite-sample behavior is only validated empirically, not proven. Theorem 3.3's exact-recovery guarantee additionally requires a minimum similarity gap delta between true and false provenance models exceeding the noise level (delta gtrsim sigma-bar/sqrt(N)); when true and false sources are not well separated (e.g., adversarially close derivatives, or very deep/attenuated lineages) the guarantee weakens. The benchmark caps model size at 3B parameters and lineage depth at 3, so scaling behavior to larger frontier models or deeper derivation chains is untested; the TAM=3 setting was dropped entirely for insufficient samples. The method assumes black-box behavioral access (greedy-decoded outputs) to both target and all candidate models over a common prompt set, and its similarity scores are themselves borrowed from prior fingerprinting work (Nikolic et al. 2025; Wu et al. 2025a) rather than proposed by this paper -- MPS's guarantees hold only relative to whatever similarity/distance function is plugged in. The authors' own Impact Statement is a one-line boilerplate disclaiming any specific societal-risk discussion, and the paper does not evaluate robustness to an adaptive adversary who might deliberately obfuscate derivation (e.g., via targeted fine-tuning to reduce measured similarity) to evade detection.

datasets_used: 455 Hugging Face LLMs (135M-3B parameters, lineage depth up to 3), collected by download count and filtered for explicit derivation links -- authors' own constructed benchmark
Bench-A (Nikolic et al., 2025): human-curated dataset of 100 provenance/non-provenance pairs plus 10 held-out reference models, extended by the authors to a multi-candidate setting
100k diverse incomplete-sentence prompts generated by ten closed-source LLMs, used to elicit behavioral fingerprints
quantitative_results: Coverage rate 96% with average predicted set size 2.1 at alpha=0.05, |M|=50, 2 true related models (Figure 2 example cited in the paper's own text)
Semantic-MPS attribution accuracy >0.97 and Token-MPS >0.95 across candidate pool sizes 10-50 (Figure 6, provenance attribution task)
MPS Precision and Recall both consistently >0.97 across pool sizes for unauthorized-derivative detection (Figure 7); baseline REEF's Recall falls to ~0.1 at |M|=50
NI-Score (non-infringement p-value) remains >0.85 for TAM=0 (non-infringing) cases and drops to near-zero for TAM>0 (infringing) cases (Figure 8)
At alpha=0.3 and |M|=100, predicted provenance set size stays below 4 (Figure 3 discussion)
Coverage stabilizes once permutation count R>=100 (Figure 4); prompt-count coverage plateaus between T=15k and T=70k depending on TAM (Figure 5)
baselines_compared: REEF (Zhang et al., 2025b) -- white-box provenance detection
LLMPrint (Hu et al., 2025) -- white-box fingerprinting via prompt injection
LLMDNA (Wu et al., 2025a) -- black-box functional-representation tracing
FWER (Nikolic et al., 2025) -- black-box model provenance testing
